### **2\. Product description**

* *4+ major features.*  
  * Foodbank guests can fill out an intake form online using google forms that uses google sheets as a temporary database.  
    * This is for proof of concept  
  * There should be a volunteer hub that allows for searching by name in the existing LFB database and uses some degree of intelligent searching  
  * Forms that contain information that could conflict with existing entries in database (duplicate addresses) should be flagged for review in our Volunteer Hub  
  * Forms that are correctly filled out with no issues should be sent directly to the existing LFB database  
* *2+ stretch goals.*  
  * Volunteer hub should allow for deleting or editing flagged entries  
  * Add a form to intake volunteers in a similar process  
  * Allow for direct edits to the LFB database within our Volunteer hub when you search something

### Abstract

	The Lynnwood Food Bank currently serves over 4,000 households and 17,400 individuals, providing vital support to our community's most vulnerable populations. However, their intake and volunteer registration processes are largely manual, relying on paper forms and in-person submissions. FoodBank Connect aims to modernize these workflows by introducing:

1. A secure, user-friendly web application that allows new guests to submit necessary forms online ahead of their visit.  
2. A secure, Volunteer Hub for processing these forms and adding them to their existing database. We would like to automate this process so it can be done easily by clicking a few buttons rather than manually copying over data.

By streamlining intake procedures, we not only help those in need with more convenient and intuitive access to the food bank, but also help volunteers focus on direct service rather than paperwork.

### **3\. Use Cases (Functional Requirements)**

| Use Case G1: Guest fills out form (Jacob) |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| Goal | Guest wishes to fill out form before arriving at foodbank |
| Primary Actor | Guest |
| Scope | Intake Form |
| Level | software (frontend) |
| Precondition | Guest has internet, guest has url |
| Success end | Guest finishes filling out form |
| Failure end | Guest unable to finish filling out form |
| Trigger | Opening form |
| Main success scenario | Guest opens up form Option to login or start new form Guest inputs email to get communications, receiving email with uid Guest fills out all fields Guest hits submit Guest receives email verifying it went through with uid they can bring to a volunteer |
| extensions | 5a. Guest submits a missing or invalid field     5a1. Guest is alerted     5a2. Guest looks through form to find missing or invalid field     5a3. Guest makes corrections     5a4. Guest hits submit |
| variations | 2\. Guest login with uid they received    2a1. logs them in and moves on to filling out fields    2b1. Show error message and guest must try again or create new form  4a. Guest receives a reminder email they didn't finish filling out the form after a day 4b. Guest receives a termination email after a week (letting them know they will need to fill out a new form).  |

![][image3]

| Use Case G2: Guest edits their information (Kyle) |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| Goal | Guest wishes to make a change to the information they submitted |
| Primary Actor | Guest |
| Scope | Google Forms |
| Level | Software (backend) |
| Precondition | Guests must have submitted a form. The form the guest submitted is incorrect in some way. |
| Success end | Guest’s updated information is correctly stored in the Food Bank Manager. |
| Failure end | Guest is unable to make modifications to their information |
| Trigger | Opening form |
| Main success scenario | Guest submits a Google form Guest realizes they want to edit their information Guest sends an email to Lynnwood Food Bank or shows up in person to notify the volunteers that they want to modify their information Volunteer edits information for the guest. |
| Extensions (error scenarios) | 3a. Guest submits the form again in attempt to modify their information        3a1. Duplicate forms are correctly identified and presented as a                 potential error in the Volunteer Hub. |
| Variations (alternative scenarios) | 4a. Guest submits the form with incorrect information    4a1. This information is presented to Volunteer Hub as potential error    4a2. Volunteer checks and modifies their information on the SoxBox database |

| Use Case V1: Volunteer checking for duplicates of guest (Jolie) |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| Goal | Volunteer wants to check if a particular individual already exists somewhere in the FBM database |
| Primary Actor | Volunteer |
| Scope | Volunteer Hub search functionality |
| Level | A step in the intake process |
| Precondition | Volunteer has logged in, navigated to the search tab, and knows who and by what parameters they wish to search by |
| Success end | Volunteer finds whether or not there is a duplicate person in the system |
| Failure end | Volunteer is unable to continue the intake process due to ambiguity |
| Trigger | Guest, through submitted online form or currently in person, has started the intake and is being processed by a volunteer |
| Main success scenario | Volunteer finds no matches at all Volunteer navigates to search bar tab Inputs the first name and/or last name and/or address of the person they want to know exists within the temporary database (in an intake form) or the FMB database (existing user) Search reveals that no matches exist Volunteer finds that a match exists but is able to speak with the guest to resolve the issue Same set up as 1 but matches exist The volunteer is there with the new guest and is able to speak with them to resolve the issue The guest realizes an account already exists for their household and uses that or they verify the matching person is not them and the volunteer proceeds to make a new account |
| extensions | Volunteer finds that a match exists but is not with the guest to confirm/deny Volunteer finds that a match exists, guest says they moved out, but the volunteer needs head of household confirmation to remove new guest from the household |
| variations | Remotely processing online intake forms vs. intaking someone in person through FBM vs intaking someone in person through their online intake submission |

![][image4]

| Use Case V2: Volunteer modifying a guests information after searching them up (Jimmy) (Not yet implemented, a part of the stretch goal for future) |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| Goal | Change guest information in database |
| Primary Actors | Volunteer, guest |
| Scope | Volunteer hub |
| Level | Volunteer subprocess |
| Precondition | Guest information is in system Guest information in system is incorrect or outdated |
| Success end | Old guest information discarded New information is saved, future fetches for this information yield the updated information |
| Failure end | Unable to find guest in system between guest and volunteer prevents process from moving forward Volunteer cannot access system (system issue/forgot password) |
| Trigger | Guest shows new information, and there is outdated or wrong information in system |
| Main success scenario | Guest comes to foodbank Volunteer confirms that they are a returning guest Volunteer searches for guest in volunteer hub using information like name, address, etc. Volunteer verifies that guest is in the system Volunteer verifies that information is correct Voluntee sees outdated or wrong information Volunteer switches to the SoxBox interface to record new information Volunteer saves this new data Next time guest comes their information is up to date with their last visit |
| extensions | Guest changes their mind about new info, change in info is cancelled |
| variations | Guest information is correct and does not need to be updated Volunteer is adding info, not changing it, for example and extra note about something In the scenario where we implement our stretch goal of editing the database through the volunteer hub, volunteer does not need to switch to SoxBox interface |

![][image5]

| Use Case V3: Guest in database talks to volunteer (Atakan) |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| Goal | Attend needs of guest, and bring requested supplies |
| Primary Actor | Volunteer |
| Scope | Volunteer Hub / Foodbank Database System |
| Level | User |
| Precondition | Volunteer is at the login screen or already logged into the Volunteer Hub. Guests information is accessible through Volunteer Hub or food bank database |
| Success end | Guest is found in database, and guest receives requested items |
| Failure end | Guest could not be located in database |
| Trigger | Volunteer logs into the Volunteer Hub. |
| Main success scenario | (If guest is not in Food Bank Database, but in temp database) Volunteer logs into the Volunteer Hub. System verifies credentials and grants access. System presents options: add guest, view/edit requests, search for guests. Volunteer selects **"Search"**. Volunteer enters guest’s information. System retrieves guest info. Volunteer communicates information with the foodbank. Foodbank provides the items requested by guest (If guest is in Food Bank Database) Follow the already existing process |
| Extensions (error scenarios) | **2a.** Login information is incorrect **2a1.** System displays: *"Username or password incorrect"* **2a2.** Volunteer is returned to the login screen. **6a.** Guest not found **6a1.** Volunteer checks in with foodbank database **6a2.** Guest is found in foodbank database and if not, guest is directly added to database and needs are met |
| Variations | – Guest is not on volunteer hub database, but is on the actual food bank database – Database contains wrong information (in which volunteer updates accordingly) |

![][image6]

|  Use Case V0: Volunteer logs-in to the website. (Isaac) |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| Goal | The volunteer wishes to gain access to sensitive data to carry out the functions of the foodbank. The foodbank guests want their data protected from third parties. |
| Primary Actor | Volunteer at Lynnwood food bank. |
| Scope | Website |
| Level | Volunteer user. |
| Precondition | The volunteer has i-pad with access to the website. |
| Success end | The volunteer has access to the website and data. |
| Failure end | The volunteer has no access to the website and no access to the data. |
| Trigger | The site is accessed by an i-pad not already logged in. |
| Main success scenario | Volunteer sees a log-in page. Volunteer writes their identification and password. Volunteer presses submit. The volunteer has access to the rest of the website. |
| extensions | 2a. Volunteer presses submit before writing all required text.   	2a1. The site will ask the volunteer for the required text.   	2a2. The volunteer will notice nothing changed and write the required text.   	2a3. Return to success scenario step 3\. 3a.  The incorrect password was entered.    	3a1. The site will inform the volunteer the password was incorrect, and ask them to try again in 10 seconds.   	 3a2. The site will not allow another response for 10 seconds.    	3a3. Return to success scenario step 1\. |
| variations | 1a. An actor who is not a volunteer at the food bank attempts to access the site instead.   	1a1. The actor would see the same log-in described in success scenario step 1\.   	1a2. The actor attempts to identify themselves.   	1a3a. If the correct password for the specified identification has been entered, then the privacy of the system is now severely compromised.   	1a3b. If the incorrect password has been entered, do not allow responses for some amount of time then return step 1a2. 2b. A volunteer chooses to log in via a *Google* account        2b1.  The volunteer presses the “Continue with Google” button and                         follows the provided instructions.        2b2. Return to success scenario step 4\.  2c. A volunteer cannot remember the password.   	2c1.  The volunteer presses a button stating “Forgot Password?”   	2c2.  Return to success scenario step 2\. |

![][image7]

### **4\. Non-functional Requirements**

Proof of Concept\- Before implementing a sophisticated frontend, we need proof that the product infrastructure will be robust.

Security\- need to ensure that data is not extractable, injectable. Information stored could have consequences if leaked. Particularly we should be wary of feedback informing users (which could include a bad actor) whether an email, address or something else is already within the system.

Scalability\- want to make it so other food banks could theoretically use the same software. We also want more features to be able to be added.

Usability\- most of the volunteers are old and have less experience with software, so needs to be easy and intuitive to use. And also take into consideration users with low vision and users with shaky hands.

Mobile compatibility\- A lot of our volunteers will be using phones and have older devices, our software needs to be mobile friendly and work well on these. In addition to being user friendly on a computer.

Testability \- The system should be structured in a way that supports thorough testing through unit and integration tests.

### **5\. External Requirements**

*Correctness*

In order to ensure the product is robust against errors that can reasonably be expected to occur, we will develop test scripts and follow strict rules in writing test functions to ensure that each function is working. In addition, we will practice abstraction allowing for the function writing to be modular and test scripts to be easily written to support the application. Those that work on the frontend will also focus on preventing user errors, following Dan Normon’s usability guidelines.

*Accessibility*

Our product will be hosted on a public URL in order for it to be usable for our client. This server will expect little traffic and we are considering making this server only locally available so ipads and computers all run together, and no risk of being raided by bots.

*Independence*

Our product will have documentation, building and hosting a server will be our first goal. It is also important that our documentation allows for those non technically experienced to run the server as our client only has one coder.

*Scope*

Our project allows for ample room for varying levels of features and functionality. We have more than 4 core features for our MVP allowing for each member to be assigned at least one feature. And we are also able to scale it back in terms of quality, while having more stretch goals to further aid volunteers.

### **6\. Team process description**

*Tools and Frameworks*

We shall use:

* *Google Forms* for guest intake because the software is trusted and our project is focusing on backend elements to demonstrate capacity.  
* *Google Sheets* for a temporary database because of its security, accessibility, and smooth integration with Google Forms.   
* *React and TypeScript* for the volunteer hub because they are robust tools the team is familiar with.  
* *Vite* as a build tool because the build tool is advertised to be compatible with *TypeScript*, to have a modern set of features, and to be fast.

*Test-Automation and CI*

Our testing framework will be ***Jest*** for the backend and ***Vitest*** for the client. 

* We are using *Jest* because it is compatible with *Vite*, our build tool, and can be used for backend testing. The API of *Jest* can be found externally at [https://jestjs.io/docs/api](https://jestjs.io/docs/api).  
* We are using *Vitest* because it better supports *Vite*.  The API of *Vitest* can be found externally at [https://vitest.dev/api/](https://vitest.dev/api/).

Tests are stored under the `__test__` folder. When adding new test files, follow the naming format \`\<element-to-test\>.spec.tsx\`. When adding tests, be sure to import the necessary functions.  One way to add a new test is to use:

test(‘A descriptive test name’ , () \=\> {

    expect(foo()).toBe(true);

});

There are more sophisticated ways of adding tests, but keep the style consistent across each testing file if doing so.

For CI, we use ***GitHub Actions***. See [11\. CI Service](#11.-ci-service) for more information.

*Members and Roles*

* *Jimmy Liu*: **Frontend follower**, **Technical writer**  
  * *Frontend* is a big aspect of this project, since we need an interface for clients and volunteers to interact directly with our software. Suited for role from experience working with React/JS in CSE 331, personal projects, hackathons, club. Will also be in charge of writing documenting frontend software.	  
* *Atakan Toprakbasti*: **Frontend Engineer**  
  * Building the frontend will likely require the knowledge of JavaScript and a framework such as React. Through the coursework of CSE331 and personal projects, I’ve gained relevant experience for this role.  
* *Kyle Boo*: **Backend \+ Database Co-lead**  
  * Storing sensitive client data using secure and scalable database systems is crucial, especially given that many individuals relying on food banks may already feel vulnerable and cautious about their personal information being exposed or misused. Has experience with full-stack development and database systems through personal projects and courseworks including CSE 331and CSE 344\.  
* *Jolie Davison*: **Backend \+ Database Co-lead, Main communicator with clients**  
  * *Backend \+ Database Co-lead* \- I enjoy connecting pieces together and UI stresses me out. I have looked into FBM’s APIs, LFB’s layout, and have a good mental model of how we will be interacting with them. I took 344 last quarter and so am excited to learn more about databases\!  
  * *Main communicator with clients* \- In starting this project, I built connections virtually and in person with LFB volunteers, tech support, and the director. Additionally, I have been in contact with FBM’s tech support to understand their API. It is easiest for me to continue these conversations given I know them and they know me.  
* *Isaac McNeill*: **Technical writing lead,** general **active follower**  
  * *Technical writing lead*: I have taken a technical writing course, have taken CSE 331, and enjoy thinking about things at a high level.  We need to be careful to create and maintain useful written artifacts to communicate to each other the system we are building, its vulnerabilities, and specify aspects of the system clearly.  I can be responsible for ensuring that our writing is effective.  
  * *Frontend active follower*: Having taken CSE 331, I have limited experience designing a website using *React*.  
  * *Backend active follower*: Having taken CSE 331 and 332, I have limited experience working with a server and thinking carefully about implementations of data structures.  
* *Jacob Seaman*: **Designer and Frontend coder**  
  * *Front End coder*: hella experienced in dart and flutter through 340 and personal projects. Have knowledge of specific libraries we would use if we went this direction. Although likely won’t. But that experience is transferable to REACT. I have experience with javascript through 331 and also appscript among other personal project. Have experience with design through doing my entire project for 440 and my personal projects which apparently require designing. Have moderate experience with Figma. 

*Tentative Schedule*

| Date Range | Milestone | Tasks | Dependencies |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| 4/29 \- 5/6 | Dummy Backend/Environment Setup/Continuous Integration Setup | Set up a dummy server for testing (1 day) Establish repo structure (2 days) Create basic CI workflow (3 days) |  |
| 5/1 \- 5/13 (Beta Release) | Guest Intake Form MVP | Design and develop form UI (6 days) Implement form fields (3 days) Tests for form validation (2 days) Create server endpoint to receive flagged entries and route properly (3 days) | Dummy Backend for testing |
| 5/1 \- 5/13 (Beta Release) | Volunteer Hub MVP | Design and develop Volunteer Hub UI (6 days) Implement login (2 days) Set up functionality for viewing, editing, searching clients (5 days) Connect to Google Sheets API to view flagged entries (1 day) Create server to access flagged entries via Google Sheets API (2 days) | Dummy Backend for testing Google Sheets connected and populated |
| 5/13-5/20 | Refine Guest Intake Form & Volunteer Hub  | Refine UI for both Guest and Volunteer (5 days) Refine logic for flagged entries (2 days)  | Routing logic complete Servers set up |
| 5/20-6/03 | Documentation & Finalize | Provide User Documentation and ensure repo contains a user manual (3 days) Provide Developer Documentation (3 days) Any final refinements (10 days) | Servers all working and able to pass data Flagging system  |

*Major Risks*

In decreasing order of concern, the major risks we have identified are that:

* If we are not able to fully develop, by week 8, the database into a state where the database can be both **accessible** and **modifiable**, then we will be forced to delay integration of frontend with backend and may not be able to produce a service that adequately meets the needs of the Lynnwood Food Bank volunteers. As a stable, robust backend is the focus of our project for CSE 403, this risk has a **low** likelihood of occurring but a **high** impact if it does.  Although we cannot reduce the impact, we plan to reduce the likelihood by actively monitoring our progress toward a stable backend.  To detect the issue, we will run automated testing.  To mitigate the risk, we can focus more of the group to the completion of the database.  
* We are unable to create an executable that starts a local server from the volunteer side. As we currently lack expertise in executable design, this risk has a **medium** likelihood of occurring and a **medium** impact if it occurs. To reduce the likelihood, we can do more research in advance. To reduce the impact, we can ensure that the documentation is clear and addresses any limitations in the executable. To detect the problem, we will carry out usability testing with contacts at the Lynnwood Foodbank and automate tests of the executable. To mitigate the issue, we can include a detailed description of our build tools in the user documentation. *Note: This executable is for the volunteers to spin-up our software without needing to understand our internal build tools.*  
* We are unable to sufficiently protect user data from leakage. Since we are collecting highly sensitive user data (names, addresses, etc), we lack knowledge regarding best practices in computer security, and governing bodies have been shown to be ruthless in their pursuit of vulnerable individuals, this risk has a **medium** likelihood of occurring but a **high** impact if it occurs. To reduce the impact, we are minimizing the extent of data we require from users via our service.  To reduce the likelihood, we will run multiple audits by experienced programmers and seek feedback from computer security specialists. To mitigate the issue, we plan to scrub cached information every 30 days to decrease the amount of data leaked should leakage occur.  
* Unforeseen misfortunes such as sudden, significant **personal difficulties**, a **meteorite**, or another **pandemic** may introduce challenges to the team members that prohibit them from effective collaboration and limit progress.  Aside from good documentation, there are **low** likelihood events that could have anywhere from **low** to **catastrophic** impact for the completion of our project. To mitigate these risks, we will maintain good internal documentation and communicate our difficulties with each other in a timely fashion.  
* We may adopt too many **features** or constraints to support within the span of CSE 403\. As the features of this project are constantly being defined in collaboration alongside volunteers of the Lynnwood Foodbank with focused yet dispersed needs, this risk has a **high** likelihood of occurring and a **low** impact. To reduce the likelihood, we seek to specify as many aspects of our service as we can. To reduce the impact, we are focusing on backend capabilities and using established software to handle the frontend.  To detect the problem, we will reference our internal documentation as we develop to prevent the spontaneous creation of features.  To fully mitigate the risk, we have resigned to the possibility that our service may not be in a state usable by our external clients by the end of the quarter.

*External Communication*

External feedback has been crucial at the beginning of development and will continue to be vital. Since we are attempting to integrate with an existing system, we need to know what the Lynnwood Food Bank needs. We have had in-person meetings, *Zoom* meetings, and many emails with different key people:

* Alissa Jones: Food Bank Director  
* Dan Wood: Main food bank tech guy  
* Cheryl Wood: Main intake volunteer  
* Don: FoodBank Manager contact

During development, we will want to double-check with Cheryl Wood that the fields we are including on the forms are exactly what they want. We will make each component as user-friendly as possible, but also want to be sure the language we are using matches how they operate.

At the beta stage, we will run a mock test of our application with Alissa Jones and Cheryl Wood to ensure that everything is to their specifications. 

Ultimately, we will be integrating our system into theirs. This may be beyond the scope of CSE 403\.

#### Documentation plan

Since usability, security, and maintainability are nonfunctional requirements for this project, we shall produce at least three sets of documentation. Specifically, we will have: 

1. a user guide guide in pdf form that graphically and succinctly guides the users of our software through each use case.   
2. a threat assessment that identifies the privacy risks of the system and explains the mitigation techniques in use.   
3. a clear, easily-navigable specifications for maintenance developers. 

### **7\. Software Architecture**

Expanding from section 2, Product Description, our major software components are:

Component 1: Intake Form  
Prompts the new client to select a language for the form to display. It will collect user data, specifically (\* means required)

* First name\* (text)  
* Last name\* (text)  
* DOB\* (DateTime)  
* Homeless\* (yes/no)  
* Street address (text)  
* Apt/Suite (text)  
* City\* (text)  
* Zip code\* (text)  
* County\* (drop-down menu)  
* Total household size, including yourself\*  
  * (size \- 1\) fields to fill in:  
    * Name\* (text), preferably first and last but just first is ok, only 1 field in FBM  
    * Age\* (text)  
* Phone number (text)  
* Primary language (drop-down menu)  
  * English, Spanish, Albanian, Amharic, Arabic, Armenian, Bengali, Burmese, Cajun, Cantonese, Chinese, Croatian, Czech, Danish, Dutch, Finnish, Formosan, French, French Creole, German, ‘Gissi, Greek, Gujarathi, Hebrew, Hindi (urdu), Hungarian, Igbo, Ilocano, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Kru, Kurdish, Lithuanian, Malayalam, Mandarin, Miao (hmong), Mon-khmer (cambodian), Navaho, Norwegian, Panjabi, Pashto, Pennsylvania Dutch, Persian, Polish, Portuguese, Qeachi, Quechua, Rumanian, Russian, Samoan, Serbocroatian, Slovak, Somali, Swahili, Swedish, Syriac, Tagalog, Thai (laotian), Tigrinya, Turkish, Ukrainian, Vietnamese, Yiddish, Yoruba  
* Nationality/race: (text)  
  * ![][image8]  
* Dietary considerations and cart additions, select as many as applicable (Checkboxes, allow multiple options)  
  * No Pork, No Beef, No Meat, No Fish/Seafood, Halal Only, No Dairy, No Regular Milk, Add Alternative Milk, No Canned Food, No Bread, No Deserts, Gluten Free Food ONLY (whole household is gluten free), Add Gluten Free (a few household members are gluten free), Add Sugar Free, Add Low Sodium Foods, Peanut Allergy, egg allergy  
  * Diapers: Diaper Wipes, Diaper size NB, Diaper size 1, Diaper size 2, Diaper size 3, Diaper size 4, Diaper size 5, Diaper size 6, Pull-ups, Adult Diapers S/M, Adult Diapers M/L, Baby Food, Baby Formula,   
  * Pets: Dog Food, Cat Food

Implemented as a Google Form. Any empty required fields will notify the user and not allow a submission.

Stores form submissions. Only accessible through the volunteer hub local server and supports decryption, and is cleared every 30 days.

Component 2: Form Validator

Validates form submissions. If the form is valid, it is sent directly to the FoodBank Manager. Otherwise, the form is sent to Volunteer Hub with a warning, indicating there is an issue with the form. Once the form is reviewed and revised by the volunteer with necessary modifications, the data can be sent to the FoodBank Manager.

An issue will be due to possible duplicate information that is already stored within the FBM. This could be due to the same addresses, addresses \+ names, and phone numbers. Will pull data from FBM to check for duplicates.

Decryption will also occur in the validation step.

Component 3: Admin manage login data

Local Node.js/Express backend/frontend that allows the foodbank to manage access rights from their volunteers for the database. Might be housed on the same server and front end as the volunteer hub, but simply an admin account with its own views and functionality.

Component 4: Volunteer Hub

Local Node.js/Express backend that reads data from Google Sheets API and exposes it to the frontend. Provides a front end for volunteers to interact with in order to view flagged form submissions and search for clients. The search by name functionality will include searches for non-head of household. With flagged submissions, an admin volunteer can edit, send the data to the FBM database, and/or delete.

Component 5: Foodbank Manager Controller

FoodBank Manager Importer:

Through the importer, the validator and volunteer hub can obtain data from external database *FoodBank Manager* without having to directly interact with the service.  This component performs API calls, organizes the results, and returns the data to its invoker.

FoodBank Manager Exporter:

Through the exporter, the volunteer hub can send data to the external database *FoodBank Manager* without having to directly interact with the service.  This component organizes arguments given, performs API calls, and returns the result to its invoker.

Will also do additional assertions to double the data is for sure stored and retrieved in the right format

Component 6: FoodBank Manager Database

This is an existing database that the Lynnwood Food Bank uses. It stores thousands of clients and their data. Volunteer Hub will use POST requests to insert new clients into this database and GET requests to search existing clients.

**Alternative Architecture Considerations**

Decision 1: Choice of Google **Sheets**

Alternative: Relational SQL database

We decided for this class to use Google Sheets for our initial development phase of the project to speed up the pace of our project. Google Sheets works seamlessly with Google Forms through various Google API’s. However, if we were to go ahead and use SQL, our database would be able to handle large amounts of data better, and have more overall control access and permissions.

Decision 2: Choice of Google **Forms**

Alternative: Custom form built using TS and React

We decided to go ahead and implement a Google Forms for our client intake form in order to speed up the development process, and have a working ‘*proof of concept*’ by the end of the class. Google Forms is very easy to work with and less time consuming as opposed to implementing our own custom form. However, if we were to use a custom form we would have full control over UI design and form behavior. 

### **8\. Software Design**

Component 1: Intake Forms

Intake Forms will use appscript and google forms to send data. In development we will utilize gasX to effectively test our functions. 

We will utilize the google api which allows upwards of 100,000 reads per day for their free service to extract data from spreadsheets from Volunteer Hub.

Component 2: Form Validator

A subcomponent of the volunteer hub. Will ensure data is accurate and flag possible problems before submitting to the database. It can use GET requests to compare to the FBM database. Will also handle decryption using an ssh key.  
Fetch Guest available columns/fields (For guest fields reference):

`curl --location --request GET 'http://{REPLACE_WITH_DOMAIN_NAME}/rest-api/clients/columns' \`  
 `-H 'Authorization: Bearer {REPLACE_WITH_USER_TOKEN}' \`  
 `-H 'Content-Type: application/json' \`

Component 3: Volunteer Hub

For the frontend, we will use React, TypeScript, to create the functional components of the website (calls to the backend and interface), and Tailwind CSS to quickly develop a somewhat aesthetic page for users to interact, allowing us to focus more on functionality and less on how the page looks. 

For the backend, we use express, and its main responsibility is to define the routes that our frontend will use. To interact with the spreadsheet our google form will be stored in, we will be using appscript and the Google API. The purpose of the backend is to abstract away the actual direct interactions with the google sheets and LFB database  
**API Request to Get User TOKEN, needed to request Guests API**

`curl 'https://{REPLACE_WITH_DOMAIN_NAME}/rest-api/token' \`  
`--header 'Content-Type: application/json' \`  
`--data '{`  
`"username": "your_user_name",`  
`"password": "your_user_pass",`  
`"location": 1`  
`}'`  
`Response:`  
`{`  
    `"status": "ok",`  
    `"code": 200,`  
    `"token": "eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9.eyJpYXQiOjE3MzgwMzU0NjIsImp0aSI6Ikg2bERTeUxFb2dxbG5TdnM1ZUFheUMzU0puUDZJRXB1XC9NVXYzRWhWUWFZPSIsImlzcyI6Im1lbHNwYW50cnkubG9jYWxob3N0OjgwODAiLCJleHAiOjE3MzgwMzkwNjIsImRhdGEiOnsidXNlcl9pZCI6MjYsImxvY2F0aW9uIjoxfX0.h-68swHoAHv6ix0uoHh20gg5-tOsNOctz_7LWZ6yd4Q"`  
`}`  
`Response:`  
`{`  
    `"status": "ok",`  
    `"code": 200,`  
    `"token": "eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9.eyJpYXQiOjE3MzgwMzU0NjIsImp0aSI6Ikg2bERTeUxFb2dxbG5TdnM1ZUFheUMzU0puUDZJRXB1XC9NVXYzRWhWUWFZPSIsImlzcyI6Im1lbHNwYW50cnkubG9jYWxob3N0OjgwODAiLCJleHAiOjE3MzgwMzkwNjIsImRhdGEiOnsidXNlcl9pZCI6MjYsImxvY2F0aW9uIjoxfX0.h-68swHoAHv6ix0uoHh20gg5-tOsNOctz_7LWZ6yd4Q"`  
`}`

Component 5: Foodbank Manager Controller:

FoodBank Manager Importer:

The FoodBank Manager Importer serves as a data retrieval interface between the internal system and the FoodBank Manager database, allowing Form Validator and Volunteer Hub to fetch relevant information through API calls.

FoodBank Manager Exporter:

The FoodBank Manager Exporter serves as a transmitter, sending valid data to the FoodBank Manager. Will use assert statements to add extra control in between queries.

Create Guest API Request:  
`curl --request POST 'https://{REPLACE_WITH_DOMAIN_NAME}/rest-api/clients' \`  
 `-H 'Authorization: Bearer {REPLACE_WITH_USER_TOKEN}' \`  
 `-H 'Content-Type: application/json' \`  
 `--data-raw '{"firstname":"Adelbert","middlename":"","lastname":"Silla","email":"","homeless":"0","blackball":"0","street_address":"Yati","apartment":"","city":"Liloan","state":"Cebu","zipCode":"","phone":"","workphone":"","cellphone":"","household_total":1,"location_id":1,"cf_guests_1f0781ed78":null,"zipcode":"6002","cf_guests_a99a503ea4":8,"cf_guests_7c4a3ed090":"test@email.com","cf_guests_92de86fbd1":"0","cf_guests_53b1b1aea7":"2"}'`

Component 6 and 1: Database(s)

For the database, we will use Google Sheets to serve as a lightweight, temporary database that stores client data from Google Forms prior to sending to the FoodBank Manager to prevent spams or invalid data. Each row corresponds to a unique client data and each column corresponds to specific form fields. This should also clear data every 30 days, and have view history turned off for view only accounts making it hopefully impossible to extract a ton of information. Although google sheets aren’t ideal for security.

### **9\. Coding Guideline**

We will use TypeScript which will support JavaScript implementation on all levels. We will use  [eslint-config-airbnb](https://www.npmjs.com/package/eslint-config-airbnb) which implements [their coding guidelines](https://github.com/airbnb/javascript). We will then use [eslint-config-prettier](https://github.com/prettier/eslint-config-prettier) which will autoformat our code to make it more legible. We will implement tests every build and before merging to maintain a functioning main branch.

Source random redditor: [https://www.reddit.com/r/typescript/comments/1aoq5gb/what\_is\_the\_best\_linting\_config\_for\_typescript/](https://www.reddit.com/r/typescript/comments/1aoq5gb/what_is_the_best_linting_config_for_typescript/) 

For appscript (if we are unable to make a stretch goal to not do a google form) we will use these [coding guidelines](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1jkxr2KdnwRAZVgoSLIJTEsLdpOMWBFuBISZ_1448tmA/edit?tab=t.0) by the genius Jacob Seaman (created 6 months ago). (there legitimately is no documentation out there on how to properly use appscript). For our testing library we will use [GasX](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1epwoUh44Dxh6wVZPrZsIuEfxLKYdHUOAdBhpeIe6iOQ/edit?gid=0#gid=0) because assert is not natively supported in appscript. And there are no linters, auto formaters or version control in google appscripts hahahahaha… welcome to hell.

### 

### **10\. Test Automation**

We use two test automation frameworks in our project. 

* For the frontend, we are using **Vitest**, which is optimized for use with Vite, our frontend build tool. It’s also ideal for our project because it’s very lightweight, fast, and TypeScript-friendly. The documentation of *Vite* can be found at [https://vite.dev/guide/](https://vite.dev/guide/).  
* For the backend, we are using **Jest**, which is ideal for our Node.js and Express server setup. We use it alongside superset for testing API routes.

To add a new test to the code base, we will follow this format:  
`import {expect, test} from 'vitest';`  
`import { demo_sum } from '../utils/utils';  // import what you want to test`

`test('A descriptive test name', () => {`  
  `expect(demo_sum(1, 2)).toBe(3)  // expects the return value of demo_sum(1, 2) to be 3`

  `// similar tests should be grouped together`  
`})`

`// other related but different tests`

### **11\. CI Service** {#11.-ci-service}

The CI service we chose is GitHub Actions. Our project is directly linked to it since GitHub Actions is already part of the GitHub platform. To set it up we will make a folder named .github/workflows and added a YAML file (ci.yml) to it. The purpose of this file is to define automated tasks, such as running builds, installing dependencies, and executing tests. GitHub Actions will run these tasks on every push to ensure the integrity of our code. The reason we went with GitHub actions is due to ease of access (it’s already part of github), not a steep learning curve as the only thing required is a simple YAML file defining different tasks, and the limits on the time to use CI services (2000 minutes per month) should not apply to us for this project. The tests that will be executed on each CI build will involve… The development action that will trigger a CI build is when we push our changes to the remote repo.

| CI Service | Pros | Cons |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| GitHub Actions | Integrated into GitHub Easy to set up Supports many languages and platforms | Limited minutes for private repos (2,000 minutes)  |
| Travis CI | Established CI tool, used very commonly Supports many languages and platforms  | Free private repo builds require a paid plan Weaker integration for GitHub as compared to Actions  |
| Circle CI | Fast builds Dashboard that shows many metrics  Free plan is 6,000 minutes a month  | Needs integration Complex configuration compared to Actions Steeper learning curve |
